description:
You are acting as a QA engineer who is preparing an isolated test environment.  
    Your goal is to start one very lightweight, never-ending background process and record its PID so that automated tools can later stop it.

    1. First, create the directory  /home/user/test_environment/pids  
       • If any intermediate directory in that path is missing, create it as well.  
       • The directory must end up with default user-level permissions (e.g., 755).

    2. Launch a single “placeholder” background process that does absolutely nothing except stay alive.  
       • Use the command  tail -f /dev/null  for this purpose.  
       • Start it **exactly once** and do NOT put any other program in the background.

    3. Immediately after starting the process, write its PID to the file  
       /home/user/test_environment/pids/daemon.pid  
       • The file must contain **only** the PID in decimal form followed by a single trailing newline character.  
       • No spaces, no other text, no extra newlines.

    4. Finally, display (cat) the newly created /home/user/test_environment/pids/daemon.pid file to the console so the test harness can capture the output.  
       The console output must therefore look like:

           12345

       …where 12345 is, of course, the actual PID of the running tail process.

    Summary of observable end state that the automated grader will verify:
       • Path /home/user/test_environment/pids exists.  
       • File /home/user/test_environment/pids/daemon.pid exists.  
       • Its contents match the regular expression  ^[0-9]+\n$  
       • Reading /proc/<that-PID>/cmdline reveals the command “tail” (i.e., the recorded PID really belongs to a tail -f /dev/null process that is still running).

    You may use whatever shell syntax you like, but everything must be achieved from the normal, unprivileged user account in a single terminal session.
truth:
The grading script will:
       1. Confirm that /home/user/test_environment/pids/daemon.pid exists.
       2. Read the file content into variable PID.
       3. Assert that PID is an integer and /proc/${PID} exists.
       4. Assert that /proc/${PID}/cmdline begins with "tail".
       5. Assert that there is ONLY ONE tail -f /dev/null process whose PID equals the number found in the daemon.pid file.
       6. Assert that the file contains exactly one newline and nothing else (len(open(file).readlines()) == 1).
name: task_000000_bf0a877f
